title: Witch Week - Chapter 5 (Fragment 4)
text:
“I don’t think there’s any point in waiting,” Miss Hodge said discontentedly. “Oh well. I can speak to him tomorrow.”

Charles was only too glad to go. He was only too glad to accompany Miss Hodge down to the door where she had left her bicycle. He talked to her most politely all the way. It kept his mind off what he had done. And he thought that if he talked hard enough and made himself truly charming, Miss Hodge might not realize that Charles had been the last person to set eyes on Mr. Wentworth.

They talked of poems, football, bicycles, the caretaker’s dog, and Mr. Hodge’s garden. The result was that Miss Hodge mounted her bicycle and rode off, thinking that Charles Morgan was a very nice child once you got to know him. It made it all the better that she intended to withdraw her accusation against him. A teacher, she told herself, should always try to get to know her pupils.

Charles puffed out a big sigh of relief and trudged off again, weighted with new guilt. By the time he reached the classroom, nearly all the others had finished their work and were trooping off to choir practice. Charles had the room to himself, apart from Nan Pilgrim, who also seemed to be behindhand. They did not speak to one another, of course, but it was doubtful that either did much work. Nan was thinking miserably that if only she was a witch like Dulcinea Wilkes, she would not mind what anyone said. Charles was thinking about Mr. Wentworth.

First the birds in music, now Mr. Wentworth. Being invisible to the senior didn’t count, because no one knew about that. What terrified Charles was that he would seem to keep using witchcraft by accident, where it showed. If only he could stop himself doing that, then he still might have a chance. Miss Hodge might give him an alibi over Mr. Wentworth, if he went on being nice to her. But how did you stop yourself working magic?

“This has been an awful day,” Nan said, as she packed up to leave. “I’m so glad it’s nearly over.”

Charles stared at her, wondering how she knew. Then he packed up and left too. He was very much afraid that today was not over for him yet, by a long way. He had heard the inquisitors usually came for you in the night. So they would come for him, as soon as someone discovered Mr. Wentworth was missing. Charles thought about Mr. Wentworth all the time he was washing. He had rather liked Mr. Wentworth on the whole. He felt very bad about him. Perhaps the way to stop himself doing it again, to Mr. Crossley or someone, was to think hard about how it felt to be burned. It would hurt.

It hurts to be burned, he repeated to himself as he undressed. It hurts to be burned. He was shivering as he climbed into bed, and not only from the cold air in the long Spartan dormitory.

Brian Wentworth was being beaten up again a few beds along from him. Brian was crouching on his bed with his arms over his head, while Simon Silverson and his friends hit him with their pillows. They were laughing, but they meant it too. “Show off!” they were saying. “Bootlicker! Show off!”

Up till then, Charles had always been almost glad he was in this dormitory, and not in the one next door like Nirupam, where Dan Smith ruled with his friends from 6C and 6D. Now he wondered whether to sneak off and sleep in the lower school boys’ playroom. Brian’s yells—for Brian could never be hit quietly—kept cutting through Charles’s miserable meditations and reminding him what he had done to Brian’s father. It grew so bothersome that Charles nearly got out of bed and joined in hitting Brian too, just to relieve his feelings. But by this time he had gathered the reason for the pillows. Mr. Brubeck had asked Brian to sing a solo at the school concert, and Brian had unwisely agreed. Everyone else knew that it was Simon’s right to sing solo.

vocabulary.definition: An unexpected event that happens unintentionally and causes damage or injury.
vocabulary.example: What terrified Charles was that he would seem to keep using witchcraft by accident, where it showed.

vocabulary.definition: To go somewhere with someone as a companion or escort.
vocabulary.example: He was only too glad to accompany Miss Hodge down to the door where she had left her bicycle.

vocabulary.definition: A charge or claim that someone has done something wrong or illegal.
vocabulary.example: It made it all the better that she intended to withdraw her accusation against him.

vocabulary.definition: A claim or evidence that one was elsewhere when an act was alleged to have taken place.
vocabulary.example: Miss Hodge might give him an alibi over Mr.

vocabulary.definition: Very bad or unpleasant.
vocabulary.example: “This has been an awful day,” Nan said, as she packed up to leave.

vocabulary.definition: Late or delayed in doing something.
vocabulary.example: Charles had the room to himself, apart from Nan Pilgrim, who also seemed to be behindhand.

vocabulary.definition: A person who tries to please others for personal gain; a sycophant.
vocabulary.example: “Bootlicker!

vocabulary.definition: Causing inconvenience or annoyance.
vocabulary.example: It grew so bothersome that Charles nearly got out of bed and joined in hitting Brian too, just to relieve his feelings.

vocabulary.definition: Attractive and pleasant; delightful.
vocabulary.example: And he thought that if he talked hard enough and made himself truly charming, Miss Hodge might not realize that Charles had been the last person to set eyes on Mr.

vocabulary.definition: A group of singers, especially in a church or school.
vocabulary.example: By the time he reached the classroom, nearly all the others had finished their work and were trooping off to choir practice.

vocabulary.definition: To lower the body close to the ground by bending the knees.
vocabulary.example: Brian was crouching on his bed with his arms over his head, while Simon Silverson and his friends hit him with their pillows.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing dissatisfaction or unhappiness.
vocabulary.example: “I don’t think there’s any point in waiting,” Miss Hodge said discontentedly.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of having done something wrong or committed a crime.
vocabulary.example: Charles puffed out a big sigh of relief and trudged off again, weighted with new guilt.

vocabulary.definition: A person who asks questions in an official or harsh manner, often to find out information or punish.
vocabulary.example: He had heard the inquisitors usually came for you in the night.

vocabulary.definition: Unable to be seen.
vocabulary.example: Being invisible to the senior didn’t count, because no one knew about that.

vocabulary.definition: The process of deep thinking or reflection.
vocabulary.example: Brian’s yells—for Brian could never be hit quietly—kept cutting through Charles’s miserable meditations and reminding him what he had done to Brian’s father.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows unhappiness or discomfort.
vocabulary.example: Nan was thinking miserably that if only she was a witch like Dulcinea Wilkes, she would not mind what anyone said.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing good manners and consideration for others.
vocabulary.example: He talked to her most politely all the way.

vocabulary.definition: A student in school.
vocabulary.example: A teacher, she told herself, should always try to get to know her pupils.

vocabulary.definition: To have control or authority over.
vocabulary.example:
”

Up till then, Charles had always been almost glad he was in this dormitory, and not in the one next door like Nirupam, where Dan Smith ruled with his friends from 6C and 6D.

vocabulary.definition: A piece or performance done by a single performer.
vocabulary.example: Brubeck had asked Brian to sing a solo at the school concert, and Brian had unwisely agreed.

vocabulary.definition: Showing the indifference to comfort or luxury traditionally associated with ancient Sparta.
vocabulary.example: He was shivering as he climbed into bed, and not only from the cold air in the long Spartan dormitory.

vocabulary.definition: Very frightened.
vocabulary.example: What terrified Charles was that he would seem to keep using witchcraft by accident, where it showed.

vocabulary.definition: To walk slowly and with heavy steps, usually because of exhaustion or harsh conditions.
vocabulary.example: Charles puffed out a big sigh of relief and trudged off again, weighted with new guilt.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner that lacks good judgment or sense.
vocabulary.example: Brubeck had asked Brian to sing a solo at the school concert, and Brian had unwisely agreed.

vocabulary.definition: To remove or take back something that was offered or said.
vocabulary.example: It made it all the better that she intended to withdraw her accusation against him.
